content.full_text:
CALIFORNIA
EPERS

Date: October 20, 1992
Memorandum
File No.: [illegible] 92

California Public Employees' Retirement System


To: Scott Hallabrim
Acting General Counsel
Legal Department
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

From: Board of Administration
Lincoln Plaza, 400 P Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Subject: Request for Formal Written Advice (Gov. Code sec. 83114(b)
and Cal. Code Regs., tit. 2, sec. 18329(b))

This is a request by the Public Employees' Retirement System
(PERS) for formal written advice pursuant to Government Code
section 83114(b), and title 2, California Code of
Regulations, section 18329(b). A statement in compliance
with Commission Regulation 18329(2)(A) is attached as
Appendix A.¹

Issues:

(1) Can PERS use the Stone opinion (3 FPPC Opn. 52) as a
basis for acceptance, by PERS, of gifts of out of
country travel/lodging, when the acceptance of such
gifts by a PERS Board member, officer, or designated
employee would be prohibited by the $250 gift
prohibition set forth in Government Code section 89505?

(2) In light of the protections against conflicts of
interest set forth in PERS' proposed travel policy, can
PERS use the Stone opinion, supra, as a basis for
acceptance, by PERS, of gifts of out-of-country
travel/lodging, when the only appropriate individuals
to use gifts are Board members or high-level PERS
officials or staff?


_____________________________

¹ During a September 28, 1992 meeting attended by you,
Carla Wardlow, Margaret Hoehn and me, you suggested that PERS
request advice in this matter in regard to positions rather
than individuals. Therefore, the compliance statement lists
the positions only.


PERS-OSS-76-HP (Rev. 1/91)

Scott Hallibrim                2                October 20, 1992


(3)   If the Stone opinion provides a basis for PERS to
      accept gifts of travel/lodging, does the attached
      proposed PERS Policy Statement Regarding Acceptance of
      Gifts comply with FPPC laws? (Appendix B.)

(4)   Does the attached proposed resolution for use by the
      PERS Board for the acceptance of gifts by PERS comply
      with FPPC laws? (Appendix C.)

General Background:

California Constitution article XVI, section 17, states that
the power of the Legislature to authorize public fund
investment is "subject to all of the following:"

      "(a) The assets of a public pension or
retirement system are trust funds and shall be
held for the exclusive purposes of providing
benefits to participants in the pension or
retirement system and their beneficiaries and
defraying reasonable expenses of administering the
system.

      "(b) The fiduciary of the public pension or
retirement system shall discharge his or her
duties with respect to the system solely in the
interest of, and for the exclusive purpose of
providing benefits to, participants and their
beneficiaries, minimizing employer contributions
thereto, and defraying reasonable expenses of
administering the system.

      "(c) The fiduciary of the public pension or
retirement system shall discharge his or her
duties with respect to the system with the care,
skill, prudence, and diligence under the
circumstances then prevailing that a prudent
person acting in a like capacity and familiar with
these matters would use in the conduct of an
enterprise of a like character and with like aims.

      "(d) The fiduciary of the public pension or
retirement system shall diversify the investments
of the system so as to minimize the risk of loss
and to maximize the rate of return, unless under
the circumstances it is clearly prudent not to do
so."

Scott Hallibrim                3                October 20, 1992


Government Code section 20205.8, found in the law which
governs PERS, essentially restates much of subsections (b)
and (c), above.

Government code section 20201 vests the exclusive control
over the administration and investment of the fund with the
PERS Board.  Government Code section 20205.6 authorizes the
Board to invest monies from the fund in any investment if
"prudent" in their informed opinion.

Government code section 20107 expressly authorizes the Board
to delegate authority to the PERS Executive Officer, and for
the PERS Executive Officer to delegate authority to PERS
staff.  Thus, the Executive Officer as well as certain high
level staff, such as the Assistant Executive Officers,
Division and Assistant Division Chiefs, PERS Investment
Office staff and Legal Office staff, are involved in the
decision making processes in regard to the administration of
the PERS System.

The PERS Board is presently participating in an
international investment program.  The action of the Board
in committing itself to a program of international
investment is part of its continuing efforts to satisfy its
constitutional duty to diversify and to maximize return on
the continuously growing funds under its control.

As noted above, the Board has the obligation to perform its
duty to make prudent investments and to diversify its
investments pursuant to the strict standards of care set
forth in the California Constitution.  In order to make
sound financial decisions on behalf of the System, the PERS
Board must high level staff members of the PERS System must
educate themselves in the complex area of foreign
investments.

On occasion, educational seminars, conferences, roundtables,
etc., in the area of international investment are offered
in various foreign countries.²  Select foreign educational
seminars offer participants the opportunity to hear
presentations by some of the world's leading experts in
international investments; to meet with high-level
government officials and business executives from countries
in which PERS is considering making, or has made


_________________________

²  Overseas sites are intentionally selected to provide
participants with first-hand knowledge of particular
international markets.

Scott Hallibrim                    4                    October 20, 1992


investments; and to gain firsthand knowledge of
international markets.

Past educational seminars offered in foreign countries have
covered topics such as:

     -    Globalization of World Economics
     -    International Equity Diversification
     -    Comparison of Fundamental Issues in International
          and Domestic Equity Investing
     -    Regional Markets (Europe, Japan and the Far East)
     -    Global Fixed-Income Investing and Strategy
     -    International Custody and Record-keeping
     -    Currency and International Investing
     -    Investment Styles and Methodology

The PERS Board has attempted to comply with its
constitutionally imposed fiduciary obligations, by adopting
a policy for the System to pay out-of-country (foreign)
educational opportunities.  However, PERS, like many other
state agencies, is subject to budget constraints, including
a reduced travel budget.  Furthermore, PERS' requests to pay
for out-of-country travel must be approved by outside
agencies, which frequently deny these requests for budget
purposes.

Thus, PERS is frequently placed in a position of being
denied the opportunity to pay the costs of sending one or
two of its Board or staff members to these out-of-country
educational seminars.

     The Impact of FPPC Gift Limitations on PERS.

As you are aware, effective January 1, 1991, FPPC laws
prohibit a member of a State board or a designated employee
of a State agency from accepting gifts with a total value of
more than $250 in a calendar year from any source that the
member or employee would be required to report as a source
of income on his/her Statement of Economic Interests.

PERS has been orally advised by the FPPC Legal Office that
the PERS Board Members, Chief Executive Officer and Chief
Investment Officer are "public officials who manage public
investments" within the meaning of Government Code
section 87200.  Thus, these individuals must report
virtually every gift, with very narrow exceptions.

Most of PERS designated employees are subject to category 1
reporting requirements under PERS' Conflict of Interest

Scott Hallibrim                 5                    October 20, 1992


Code.  (This Code is attached as part of Appendix A.)  Their
reporting requirements are also very broad.

PERS is aware that the following travel and accommodations
may be accepted without limitation, need not be reported on
the member's or employee's Statement of Economic Interests:

     (1)  Travel and reasonable accommodations that are provided
          by the member's or designated employer's governmental
          agency;

     (2)  Travel and reasonable accommodations that are paid by a
          source not required to be disclosed on a member's or
          designated employee's Statement of Economic Interests.
          (Gov. Code sec. 89506.)

PERS is further aware that pursuant to section 89506,
acceptance of payments, advances or reimbursements for
travel, including actual transportation, and related lodging
and subsistence, are not subject to the absolute ban on the
acceptance of honorarium, or the gift limits, if either of
the following criteria are met:

     (1)  The travel is reasonably related to a legislative or
          governmental purpose, or to an issue of state,
          national, or international, public policy; AND

          The travel is in connection with giving a speech; AND

          The travel is within the United States; OR

     (2)  The travel is reasonably related to a legislative or
          governmental purpose, or to an issue of state,
          national, or international public policy; AND

          The travel is provided by one of the following:  a
          government, a governmental agency, a foreign
          government, a governmental authority, a bonafide public
          or private education institution as defined by the
          Internal Revenue Code, a tax exempt nonprofit
          charitable or religious organization, or a person
          domiciled outside of the United States which
          substantially satisfies the requirements for tax exempt
          status under 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code.

Most of the foreign educational conferences are sponsored by
entities that would be reportable sources of income for
both:  (1) PERS Board Members/Officers who report under
Government Code section 87200 et seq. on the Form 721; and

Scott Halibrim                    6                    October 20, 1992


(2) PERS designated employees who report under PERS'
Conflict of Interest on the Form 730.

On occasion, the sponsor of a foreign educational seminar
has offered to pay the travel/lodging expenses of a PERS
Board or staff member so that they can attend.  However,
because of the $250 gift limitation from a reportable source
in a calendar year, PERS Board Members, officers and
designated employees have been unable to accept these
offers.

PERS' inability to pay for foreign travel/lodging of its
Board Members, officers or employees, combined with the
inability of these individuals to accept gifts aggregating
$250 in a calendar year from a reportable source, is
interfering with PERS' ability to carry out its fiduciary
obligations in the area of foreign investments.  Not only
does PERS lose opportunities to gain necessary and required
knowledge and understanding in the international investing
market, but PERS is also unable to exchange and share
investment concerns regarding the international investment
market with other retirement fund representatives who are in
attendance.

Therefore, PERS is respectfully seeking FPPC guidance
regarding developing procedures that would permit PERS,
rather than individuals, to accept gifts of foreign
travel/lodging primarily in connection with PERS' investment
responsibilities.

Protections From Conflict of Interest Offered
By PERS' Proposed Travel Policy.

The PERS Board is in the process of developing a written
policy statement regarding travel by members of the Board
and staff.  PERS forwarded two different proposed drafts of
travel policy statements to the FPPC for review in July
1992.  The FPPC provided PERS with an informal advice
letter.  (FPPC File No. I-92-392.  The request for advice
and FPPC response are attached as Appendix D.)

Pursuant to the June 29, 1992 version of PERS' proposed
travel policy, the President of PERS' Board of
Administration would be required to approve all travel for
Board Members and the Chief Executive Officer when the
travel would require use of PERS funds or use of funds
provided by any source that must be disclosed on the
individual's Statement of Economic Interests.  Furthermore,
approval of travel requests would be limited as follows:

Scott Hallibrim                    7                    October 20, 1992


"1. The Chief Executive Officer shall be
eligible to attend as many conferences each year
as deemed necessary and appropriate by the
President of the Board of Administration.

"2. Each Board Member shall be eligible to
attend a maximum of three (3) conferences per
year, not including:

"a. Conferences held within the
State of California;

"b. Conferences where the Board
Member has been requested to participate
as a speaker." (See p. 3 of the
June 29, 1992 version of PERS' proposed
travel policy.)

The June 29 version further states that "[the Board
President shall not approve attendance for more than two
Board members at any one conference.³" (See p. 3.)

The proposed May 1992 version contains similar restrictions
on travel by PERS Board Members and staff.

As can be seen from these proposed travel policy statements,
PERS is interested in travel only when it is necessary for
the proper administration of this system.

Confusion Regarding PERS' Ability to Accept Gifts
Pursuant to the Stone Opinion.

In a June 9, 1977 opinion, the FPPC concluded that where a
gift is deemed to have been made to the agency, rather than
to any official, the official has "no reporting obligation
since whatever he receives, although free of charge to both
him and the . . . [agency] would be analogous to
reimbursement for expenses or per diem from a state or local
government agency, items which are not reportable. Section
82030(b)(2)." (The Stone opinion, 3 FPPC opn. 52, pp. 5-6.)

The Stone opinion further states:

"While no immutable guidelines can be cast
for determining when a gift of this nature is a

_____________________

³ This prohibition would not apply to conferences at
which the Board President has called for a noticed meeting of
the full Board of Administration.

Scott Hallibrim                8                October 20, 1992


     gift to the city only, and not the official, we
would require it to satisfy at least the following
four criteria:

     "1. The donor intended to donate the gift to
the city and not the official;

     "2. The city exercises substantial control
over use of the gift;

     "3. The donor has not limited use of the gift
to specified or high level employees, but rather
has made it generally available to city personnel
in connection with city business without regard to
official status; and

     "4. The making and use of the gift was
formalized in a resolution of the city council (a
written public record will suffice for
administrative agencies not possessing the
legislative power of adopting resolutions) which
remedies the standards set forth above."
(Emphasis added; Id., at p. 6.)

The Stone opinion also indicates that the circumstances
surrounding the gift must be reviewed to determine whether
the gift was made to the agency, "without providing any
significant or unusual benefit to the official." (Id., at
p. 5.)

The PERS Legal Office has received various oral advice from
the FPPC regarding the continued viability of the Stone
opinion, and PERS' ability to use it as a basis for PERS to
accept gifts of travel/lodging in connection with travel
outside of the United States.

PERS has received oral advice from the FPPC that the Stone
opinion, supra, was written well before the enactment of the
new ethics laws which prohibit the acceptance of honorarium
and place limits on the acceptance of gifts, and was not
intended to permit public officials to circumvent these
prohibitions and limits by having the agency accept gifts.

However, PERS has also been told that the Stone opinion,
supra, could be used as a basis for PERS to accept gifts of
lodging and travel in situations where PERS Board Members,
officers, and designated employees would be prohibited from
doing so by the $250 gift limitations.

Scott Hallibrim                9                October 20, 1992


Therefore, PERS respectfully requests clarification
regarding the current viability of the Stone opinion.

PERS has also been orally informed by the FPPC that if the
donor really intends for a gift to be used by a particular
individual, or member of a certain limited class, such a
member of the Board or Executive staff, then the gift is not
truly to the agency within the meaning of the Stone opinion,
and the gift may be treated by the FPPC as one to the
individual. PERS was further informed that it would be a
simple matter for the FPPC to ascertain whether gifts
accepted by an agency are being given only to Board Members
or high level staff, notwithstanding any resolution adopted
by the agency which contains "boiler-plate" language to the
effect the donor has not limited the use of the gift to any
particular person or to high level employees. It was
suggested that, based on the pattern of actual use, the FPPC
may determine that the gifts of travel/lodging were really
given to or accepted by certain individuals, rather than by
the agency.⁴

This later advice has caused considerable confusion and
difficulty for PERS, because in the normal course of
business, foreign trips would only be taken by high level
staff or PERS Board Members. These trips appropriately
would be taken only by the "decision makers" of the System.
The purpose of these trips is to provide necessary and
required education regarding the international investment
market to its Board Members and high level staff. PERS
would have no legitimate business reason to send individuals
who did not occupy some high-level decision making powers.
Furthermore, it is unlikely that donors would want to give
PERS gifts of travel/lodging for a foreign educational
seminars if they knew PERS would be required on an occasion
to send staff who possessed no decision making authority, in
order to satisfy a technical FPPC requirement.

Thus, PERS respectfully requests that the FPPC provide PERS
with written clarification in regard to the requirement in
the Stone opinion that ". . . the donor has not limited use
of the gift to specified or high level employees, but rather
has made it generally available to city personnel in
connection with city business without regard to official
status."

_____________________

     ⁴ This could have significant consequences for the
particular individual who utilized the gift, if the gift
falls within the scope of the new limitations and
prohibitions.

Scott Hallibrim          10          October 20, 1992


The PERS Legal Office respectfully suggests that if the
donor does not restrict the use of the gift to specified or
high level employees, this requirement of the Stone opinion
has been satisfied. PERS respectfully notes that the Stone
opinion does not contain any discussion regarding "pattern
of actual use." As noted above, if this approach were taken
by the FPPC, it could result in an inappropriate use of the
gift on occasion. Furthermore, it could create notice/due
process problems. For example, at what point has a pattern
of use been established?

PERS submits that it should be left to PERS to determine the
appropriate person(s) to use any gift of travel/lodging.
PERS has the intimate knowledge of how this System operates
and what is needed for its administration in the usual
course of business. PERS further respectfully submits that
its suggested approach would be consistent with the FPPC's
position that gifts accepted by an agency must be the types
of services or items which PERS would provide normally in
the usual course of its business.

If the Stone opinion, supra, does provide a basis for PERS
to accept gifts of travel/lodging, do the attached proposed
PERS Policy Statement Regarding Acceptance of Gifts
(Appendix B) and the proposed resolution for use by the
acceptance of gifts (Appendix C) comply with FPPC laws?


RICHARD H. KOPPES
General Counsel

RHK:jks

Attachments
